lesson.title: Arc Welding (SMAW/MMA)
lesson.explanation: Shielded Metal Arc Welding is the most versatile and widely-used welding process in SA. An electric arc (50-300A) melts the electrode and base metal. Electrode coating provides shielding gas and slag protection. Common electrodes: E6013 (general purpose, easy), E7018 (structural, low hydrogen). Joint types: butt, lap, tee, corner. Positions: 1G flat, 2G horizontal, 3G vertical, 4G overhead. Safety: welding helmet (shade 10-13), leather gloves, apron, ventilation. AWS D1.1 and SANS standards apply.

practice_problems.question: You need to weld two 10mm mild steel plates in a butt joint. Select the appropriate electrode (AWS classification), diameter, polarity, and approximate amperage. Explain your choices.
practice_problems.answer: Electrode: E7018 (low-hydrogen, basic-coated) for structural mild steel. Diameter: 3.2mm for root pass, 4.0mm for fill/cap passes. Polarity: DCEP (reverse polarity) for deeper penetration. Amperage: root 90-110A (3.2mm), fill/cap 130-160A (4.0mm). Rule of thumb: 30-40A per mm of electrode diameter. Joint prep: 60-70° V-groove, 1.5-2mm root face, 2-3mm gap.
hints: Low-hydrogen electrodes must be stored in a rod oven at 120°C after opening.
practice_problems.explanation: Electrode selection, amperage, and joint prep determine weld quality before striking an arc. E7018 is the most specified electrode in SA structural fabrication.
distractors: E6013 is better for structural work

practice_problems.question: Visual inspection reveals: (a) undercut along the weld toe, (b) a crater crack at the stop point. Identify causes and corrective actions for each.
practice_problems.answer: (a) Undercut: groove melted into base metal at weld toe. Causes: excessive amperage, fast travel speed, incorrect electrode angle, long arc. Fix: reduce amperage 10-15%, slow travel, pause at toes during weaving. Repair undercut >1mm with a cosmetic pass. (b) Crater crack: crack at the stop crater due to shrinkage stress. Fix: use backstep technique (reverse 10-15mm before breaking arc) or crater-fill technique. Grind out existing cracks and re-weld.
hints: Undercut creates stress concentrations where fatigue cracks initiate.
Crater cracks are 100% preventable with proper stopping technique.
practice_problems.explanation: Visual inspection and defect identification are the first quality control steps. Understanding causes enables prevention rather than repair.
distractors: Grind the entire weld out to fix undercut

practice_problems.question: You are welding overhead (4G) on a structural beam. What adjustments do you make compared to flat position (1G)?
practice_problems.answer: Adjustments: (1) Reduce amperage 10-15% -- gravity pulls the pool down. (2) Use 3.2mm max electrode for pool control. (3) Short arc length (1-2mm). (4) Narrow stringer beads, no wide weaving. (5) Slightly faster travel to keep pool small. (6) 5-10° push angle. Why hardest: gravity pulls molten pool down, demanding precise heat/speed/arc control. Slag inclusion risk increases as pool solidifies against gravity.
hints: Key principle: keep the weld pool SMALL in overhead.
Overhead is mandatory for most structural welding qualifications.
practice_problems.explanation: Positional welding separates qualified welders from beginners. SANS 2001-CS1 requires testing in the positions the welder will use.
distractors: Use same settings as flat position

practice_problems.question: A client needs a repair weld on a cast iron engine block. What special considerations, electrode, and procedure apply?
practice_problems.answer: Cast iron is brittle (2-4% carbon, low ductility). Electrode: ENiFe-CI (nickel-iron) -- NOT E7018, which causes brittle carbon pickup. Procedure: (1) Clean by grinding. (2) Preheat 200-300°C. (3) Low amperage (minimum to maintain arc). (4) Short runs (25-30mm), peen each pass with ball-peen hammer while hot (compresses weld, counters shrinkage). (5) Slow-cool wrapped in insulating blanket -- NEVER quench. This prevents martensite formation in the HAZ.
hints: Cardinal rule: minimum heat, slow cooling, peen after each pass.
Cast iron fails when welders use wrong electrode or cool too fast.
practice_problems.explanation: Cast iron repair is specialist SMAW work requiring metallurgy knowledge and practical technique.
distractors: Use E7018 -- steel electrodes work on everything
Preheat is unnecessary for small repairs

practice_problems.question: Compare E6013 and E7018 electrodes. A shop uses only E6013 for all work. Should they switch to E7018 for structural welding?
practice_problems.answer: E6013: rutile coating, easy to use, 60ksi tensile, uncontrolled hydrogen. Good for general light fabrication and beginners. E7018: low-hydrogen basic coating, 70ksi, excellent ductility and impact toughness, requires DCEP and rod oven storage. Required for structural/code work. The shop MUST switch for structural welding: (1) E6013 doesn't meet SANS 2001-CS1 requirements. (2) Need rod oven investment (R3-5K). (3) Welder retraining on E7018 handling. (4) Higher electrode cost but superior weld integrity. E7018 exposed >4 hours to atmosphere must be re-baked at 350°C for 1 hour.
hints: The '8' in E7018 = low-hydrogen coating, critical for preventing hydrogen cracking.
Hydrogen-induced cracking can appear hours after welding.
practice_problems.explanation: Electrode selection must meet code requirements. Using E6013 on structural work is a code violation with potential for catastrophic failure.

misconceptions.misconception: You don't need formal training for Arc Welding (SMAW/MMA)
misconceptions.correction: Proper training and certification are required for safety and legal compliance in SA.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: People see informal work and assume no qualifications are needed.
